metadata.title: Build a Signal Collapse Detector with Claude
metadata.purpose: Show students in Nina Harris' Branding and AI course how to build a CLI tool that scores credential signal strength across Cost-to-fake, Population-saturation, and Status dimensions, exposing which portfolio artifacts have pooled and what costly signals still separate.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — the master clock; every beat conforms to its measured audio
metadata.audience: Students and practitioners in a university branding course with an AI component
sources: Branding and AI, Chapter 1 — The Creative Engineer (Nina Harris)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'ClaudeComposerAsk916' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
END: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: Bonjour — this is Liam, in for Bear. For Nina Harris' course, Branding and AI, here is the question we are running today: your GitHub repo used to mean something. Now eighty-two percent of developers use AI tooling and everyone has one. The signal has pooled. So we are building a tool that tells you which credentials still separate and which ones have already collapsed.

props.command: Score this credential: GitHub repo, software engineering 2024. Rate Cost-to-fake (1–5), Population-saturation (1–5), Status (Separating/Pooling/Collapsed). Recommend one costly signal that still separates.
output: Cost to fake: 2 (AI tooling collapsed it)
Recommend: deployed product with real users

beats.narration_text: Here is what breaks without this tool. You invest months building a credential that felt prestigious three years ago. The market has already repriced it. You find out at the interview. Spence's signaling model says a credential works only as long as its cost structure holds. AI tooling just collapsed the cost structure for a dozen of the most common ones simultaneously.

props.sparkLine: A signal works only as long as its cost structure holds.

beats.narration_text: The theory from Chapter One. Michael Spence's model: a signal separates candidates only when it is costly enough that low-productivity candidates cannot afford to fake it. Three dimensions carry the diagnostic: Cost to fake — how many genuine months does this take? Population saturation — what share of candidates produce it? And status — is it still sorting, has it pooled, or has it already collapsed? A GitHub repo, 2024: cost collapsed to near-zero, saturation at five, status: collapsed.

The centerpiece: this is the tool's output for a GitHub repo in software engineering 2024. Cost to fake scores two — AI tooling brought this down dramatically. Population saturation scores five — Stack Overflow data puts AI adoption at eighty-two percent of developers. Status reads Collapsed. The terracotta cell is where the signal died. Below the matrix: the tool recommends the next costly signal that would restore separation — in this case, a deployed product with real users, because the cost structure there has not yet pooled. Source: Branding and AI, Nina Harris, Chapter One.

Honesty about what this tool does not do. Signal value is context-specific and changes over time — a credential that has collapsed in software engineering may still separate in a different field. The tool is a diagnostic, not a verdict. And the recommendation is a starting point, not a guarantee — the next costly signal will pool eventually too. The tool gives you the map, not permanent terrain.

artifactLines: Signal value is context-specific — results differ by field and year.
The tool is a diagnostic, not a permanent verdict.
Recommendations are starting points — every signal pools eventually.
The map is not the terrain.

beats.narration_text: Let us recap with Claude. Signal Collapse Detector. Four lines.

artifactLines: A credential's value is its cost structure, not its name.
Claude scores three dimensions and surfaces the collapse.
The matrix shows where your signal has already pooled.
The next costly signal is where you invest instead.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take this prompt into your Claude session: I want you to act as a Spence signaling analyst. I am a student in the field of blank, graduating in blank year. Score each of these three credentials I plan to invest in: blank, blank, blank. For each, rate Cost-to-fake one to five, Population-saturation one to five, and Status — Separating, Pooling, or Collapsed. Then recommend the one costly signal in my specific field that is still separating right now, with a rationale grounded in Spence's mechanism. Run this on your actual field and your actual credentials. The tool forces you to name the cost structure before you invest. That is the whole discipline.

props.command: Act as a Spence signaling analyst. I'm a student in [FIELD], graduating [YEAR]. Score these three credentials I plan to invest in: [CREDENTIAL 1], [CREDENTIAL 2], [CREDENTIAL 3]. Rate each: Cost-to-fake (1–5), Population-saturation (1–5), Status (Separating/Pooling/Collapsed). Recommend the one costly signal in my field that is still separating now, with a Spence rationale.

beats.narration_text: Build a Signal Collapse Detector with Claude. Liam, in for Bear.

props.title: Build a Signal Collapse Detector with Claude
